questions.description: Following the enactment of the Companies Act 2014, the doctrine of Ultra is no longer of central importance to…
questions.text: Following the enactment of the Companies Act 2014, the doctrine of Ultra is no longer of central importance to questions of corporate capacity.' You are required to discuss and explain this statement. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Emma and David are the directors and equal shareholders in Fresh Air… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Emma and David are the directors and equal shareholders in Fresh Air Limited (the 'Company'), which operates an outdoor adventure business based in the west of Ireland. The company owns approximately 50 acres of mountainous terrain on which it operates its business. An opportunity has arisen to acquire a neighbouring mountain from Mountain Men Ltd, a company which is owned 50:50 by David and his brother Bart. Mountain Men Ltd has agreed to sell the mountain to the Company (Fresh Air Limited) for €400,000 or lease the mountain to the Company for 20 years at a market rent of €15,000 per annum. You are required to advise the Company and Mountain Men Ltd on their obligations under (and only under): (a) Section 238 of the Companies Act 2014; and (b) Section 239 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: 'In limited liability companies, dividends can only be declared from… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: 'In limited liability companies, dividends can only be declared from distributable profits, and assets can only be transferred to shareholders at an under value when the company has sufficient distributable profits. You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Sean is a wood turner who has operated as a sole trader for the last 5… Company.
questions.text: Sean is a wood turner who has operated as a sole trader for the last 5 years, making wooden bowls and assorted other goods from wood for the tourist market. He employs six people and his business has been very successful. Sean comes to you for advice and during the course of the consultation; he tells you a number of things about his business: - He is considering incorporating the business but is not sure of the advantages and disadvantages of doing this. - He is about to acquire a new factory outlet, whereby the products will be made in the same location as they are sold, and to where members of the public can come and see him and his employees at work. Sean will need to borrow from his bank to acquire the new premises. - Recently, one of his employees received a nasty cut from a chisel which severed an artery, and caused him to be off work for two months and, although Sean's insurance company paid out on the claim, Sean had a few sleepless nights worrying over the claim until he received their letter confirming it was covered in full by the policy. - Sean's longest serving employee, Fred, has for some time been pressing Sean to form a partnership with him so he can share in the success of the business. While Sean is agreeable to giving Fred a sluice of action, he is very uncomfortable with forming a partnership with Fred. - Although Sean has a book-keeper, he has never bothered having his accounts audited. You are required to advise Sean on the [Advantages and Disadvantages]{.underline} of incorporating his business as a private company limited by shares under Part 2 of the Companies Act 2014, having particular regard to the matters raised by Sean during the consultation. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: While a creditor of a company is entitled ex debito justiciae (as a matter of right) to a winding-up order, the remedy…
questions.text: While a creditor of a company is entitled ex debito justiciae (as a matter of right) to a winding-up order, the remedy of winding up is discretionary remedy and is not available in cases of disputed debts'. You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Richard and Frank are equal shareholders and directors in RF Events Limited (the 'Company') which has been in the…
questions.text: Richard and Frank are equal shareholders and directors in RF Events Limited (the 'Company') which has been in the business of organising corporate events since 2005. Business was generally very strong for the last number of years, and while the Company has a core staff of 12 employees, at times it employed up to 30 people. In the last year business has never been worse. Clients are either not hosting events or are cancelling events already planned. Richard and Frank have cut staff salaries by 20% and have stopped taking a salary for themselves. Even so, the Company is struggling to keep afloat and is behind on both the rent for its premises and its bank loan of €2,500,000. The bank loan is secured by a mortgage debenture incorporating a floating charge and a fixed charge of the Company's premises, which is now worth only €1,750,000. This month it is unlikely to be able to pay the Revenue Commissioners the full amount of VAT that the Company collected from its customers, having used it to pay wages. Richard and Frank believe that, if the Company can only keep afloat for the next 6 months, it stands a good chance of surviving, as they believe that the worst of the effects of Brexit and the uncertainties in the USA, have both stabilised. Frank has approached and old business colleague, now retired, and asked him to become an investor in the company, offering him 25% of the Company (12½% each from him and Richard). Matters came to ahead yesterday when the Company's bank appointed a receiver on account of the Company's bank loan being in arrears. Richard and Frank approach you for advice on: (a) The steps involved in bringing a petition to have an examiner appointed to the Company; (b) The likelihood of success of a petition to have an examiner appointed to the Company; (c) The consequence for the Company and its creditors of an examiner being appointed; and (d) The duties and powers of an examiner. You are required to advice Richard and Frank in relation to the matters set out at (a), (b), (c) and (d) which should be clearly distinguished in your answer. You are required to refer to all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: "The causes of crystallisation of a floating charge are now well settled in Irish case law".
questions.text: "The causes of crystallisation of a floating charge are now well settled in Irish case law". You are required to explain the legal nature of a floating charge and to discuss the above statement. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Freddie is a sole director and shareholder in Freddie's Freakish Fish… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Freddie is a sole director and shareholder in Freddie's Freakish Fish Limited ("the company") which operates a pet shop in Naas specialising in the sale of exotic fish and related items such as fish tanks, fish food etc. During the last 6 months the Company's business had been going downhill and Freddie, seeing the writing on the wall, did a number of things: (a) He removed stock to the value of €50,000 from the Company's premises, and purported to sell it to his wife Freda who has her own pet shop in Kildare, although it does not appear that Freda paid for the stock; (b) He caused the Company to pay off a debt of €75,000 which had been loaned to it by his father, Jeremy. Last month the Revenue Commissioners (who are owed €250,000) successfully petitioned the High Court to have a liquidator appointed. The liquidator approaches you for advice on the remedies open to him under the Companies Act 2014 in relation to the two dispositions identified at (a) and (b). Advise the liquidator. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Referring to the principle that a company is a legal entity distinct from its members Lardner J stated "the statutory…
questions.text: Referring to the principle that a company is a legal entity distinct from its members Lardner J stated "the statutory rules of company law in which the principle is implicit apply to the relationship between holding companies and subsidiaries and to transactions between them and third parties". You are required to discuss this application of the principle of separate corporate personality in the specific context of holding companies and subsidiaries, citing all relevant case and stature law in your answer.

questions.description: "It is now well recognised that a company's members can give their sanction to a course of action in either of three…
questions.text: "It is now well recognised that a company's members can give their sanction to a course of action in either of three ways: first, by a resolution passed at a general meeting of the company, duly convened and held; secondly, by unanimous or majority written resolution and thirdly, by an estoppel, created through their unanimous informal agreement." You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: For the last 6 years Jackie operated a florist shop from a unit in a… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Jackie's business has failed. For the last 6 years Jackie operated a florist shop from a unit in a shopping centre outside of Dublin. The business was conducted through Smell That DAC (the "Company") of which Jackie was the sole shareholder and one of two directors, the other director being Jackie's former fiancé, Ted. Jackie contacted Ted to tell him the company was insolvent, and they agreed to hold a directors' meeting with a view to putting the Company into creditors' voluntary winding up. On subsequently meeting with Jackie and Ted, you elicit the following information: - The Company now owed €500,000 in back rent and taxes. The Company has struggled through the recession, as people have had less disposable income to spend on flowers. The final straw has been an increase in rent in relation to the shop unit that the Company rents, as it was increased from €20,000 to €40,000 per month. - The Revenue Commissioners have not been paid VAT on sales, or the PRSI or PAYE due on Jackie's salary from the Company from three months. - In addition, the Revenue Commissioners have issued an assessment from €50,000 in relation to back tax owed to it, which the Company cannot afford to pay. The circumstances in which this has become due were that Jackie had hired a teenage girl to work a few hours on a Saturday morning, paying her cash in hand. When this was discovered by the Revenue Commissioners, they claimed for lost PRSI and PAYE tax on girl's earnings over a two-year period. - The Company's books and records have been maintained by a local book-keeper to an acceptable standard, however, on the way to delivering the books and records to the book-keeper for the last 12 month period, Jackie's car was stolen and the books and records destroyed after the joy-rider thieves set fire to the car. - Ted, the Company's second director, has not been active in the business of the Company. He says he agreed to be a director whilst engaged to Jackie because every company needs two directors, but he has not been active in the Company's business, only signing accounts and forms when asked to do so. Although they have since split up, it was amicable, and Ted only agreed to stay on until Jackie could find someone else to become a director. - The liquidator has been looking to meet with them both, but to date only Jackie has met with him. Ted left a message with the liquidator's office to say "there is nothing I can help you with". You are required to advise both Jackie and Ted on the likelihood that they will be restricted under section 819 of the Companies Act 2014 and in particular on the defences available to them. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Bob, Patrick, and Patty are directors of B-P-P Limited (the "Company")… Company, Shareholder.
questions.text: Bob, Patrick, and Patty are directors of B-P-P Limited (the "Company") which runs an estate agency. They are also shareholders in the Company and each hold 25 of the 100 issued shares, the other 25 shares being held by John. John is a solicitor and has never been a director of the company, being content to be just a shareholder. The premises from which the Company operates is owned by John and leased to the Company at a very favourable rent for the Company. Bob, Patrick and Patty have traditionally recommended that purchasers and vendors of properties that go through the agency use John's solicitors practice for their conveyancing and this has been a very lucrative source of revenue to John. John would in turn recommend to his clients that they use the Company as their estate agent. In recent times, however, John's recommendations have been few and far between and Bob, Patrick and Patty have been recommending other firms of solicitors to clients of the Company as it seems to them that these other solicitors are in a better position than John to make cross-referrals back to the Company. On average, the Company would have made about 30 referrals to John every month; however, for the last 6 months, this has fallen steadily and last month, there were only 5 referrals to him. John was unsure as to why referrals were falling off and had blamed it on the economic down turn until one of his friends in another firm of solicitors (who did not know John was a shareholder in the Company) mentioned that he and the Company were cross-referring clients very successfully. John was very annoyed and confronted Bob, Patrick and Patty, claiming that in ceasing to maintain his level of referrals they were acting in disregard of his interests as a shareholder and pointing to the below market rent he charged the Company for its premises. John also objected to not being told about their decision to make referrals elsewhere. Their response was that they were acting in the interests of the Company and had never asked for a lower rent and that he was welcome to seek to review the rent if he thought it was not at market value. They also indicated that if this was John's attitude, they would not make any referrals to him, a statement which provoked John to claim that they were acting oppressively. You are required to advise on the following matters: (a) whether John has a remedy under section 212 of the Companies Act 2014; and (b) whether your answer would be different if the Company's constitution or a shareholder's agreement contained a provision that the Company would support shareholders' businesses by referring clients to them. You are required to answer both part (a) (which carries 15 marks) and part (b) (which carries 5 marks). In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "Section 158(1) of the Companies Act 2014 has replaced Regulation 80 of Part 1 of Table A to the Companies Act 1963 as…
questions.text: "Section 158(1) of the Companies Act 2014 has replaced Regulation 80 of Part 1 of Table A to the Companies Act 1963 as the cornerstone of corporate governance." You are required to discuss this statement, and in answering this question you must cite all relevant case and stature law in support of your answer.

questions.description: Irish Books Limited (the "Company") is an on-line book shop operating from… Mortgage, Company.
questions.text: Irish Books Limited (the "Company") is an on-line book shop operating from an industrial estate in West Dublin. The Company has a term loan and overdraft from Big Bank plc (the "Bank") secured by a mortgage debenture incorporating a first charge over its premises. Its borrowings are also secured by a separate fixed charge over its book debts. Book debts are paid into a separate account which the Bank can freeze at its discretion. Due to poor turnover, the Company stopped repaying its loans to the Bank which than appointed a receiver. Last month the directors placed the Company into creditors' voluntary liquidation. Following his appointment, the Company's liquidator inspected the security which the Bank holds for its loans to the Company. The liquidator tells you that: (a) through an oversight, the mortgage debenture in favour of the Bank comprising the fixed charge over its premises which contained the power to appoint the receiver, was [not]{.underline} registered in the Companies Registration Office under section 409 of the Companies Act 2014; and (b) while the charge over the book debts was registered under section 409, however, the charge created over a bank account into which book debts was paid was not registered under section 409. The liquidator also tells you that the Revenue Commissioners are claiming priority of the due to them ahead of sums due to the Bank. You are required to advise the liquidator on the enforceability of the security described in (a) and (b) [which should be clearly distinguished in your answer as both parts carry equal marks.]{.underline} In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "While company directors have a fiduciary duty to act in the interests of their company, they also have duties to have…
questions.text: "While company directors have a fiduciary duty to act in the interests of their company, they also have duties to have regard to the interests of other persons; however, all such duties are owed to the company." You are required to discuss and explain this statement. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: David, Deborah and Cindy are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in Foxtrot Tango Limited (the "Company"),…
questions.text: David, Deborah and Cindy are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in Foxtrot Tango Limited (the "Company"), established by their late mother Madge. They received their shares from Madge under her will. The Company is in the business of retailing carpet and its only two directors are David and Deborah who also work in the Company. Cindy left the country a number of years ago and lives with her husband in San Francisco and over the years has not had any involvement in the Company. Once a year she contacts her brother and sister to ask how the Company is doing and usually, following that call, David and Deborah declare a dividend which the Company pays to Cindy, averaging over €50,000 per annum for the last three years. Last Christmas when Cindy came home to Ireland she tentatively enquired from David and Deborah whether they would be interested in buying her shares in the Company. They said they would have to think about it because neither had cash on hand to buy her shares. David and Deborah come to you for advice when Cindy contacted them again and announced that she had sold her shares in the Company to Joe, a local businessman in the same town as where the Company has its premises. A couple of days later David and Deborah were approached by Joe who gave them a stock transfer form signed by Cindy transferring her 50 shares to him, and he asked them to register him as a member. David and Deborah are unhappy to share the family business with an outsider and ask you for your advice. On examination of the Company's constitution you see that the optional provision set out in section 95(1)(a) of the Companies act 2014 applies and that the directors may in their absolute discretion and without assigning any reason for doing so, decline to register any transfer of any share.
